sections.text: Welcome back to AP Biology — Module Seven, DNA Structure and Replication. This kicks off College Board Unit Six, Gene Expression and Regulation, which is a big one — twelve to sixteen percent of the AP exam. Today we look at what DNA actually is, and how every one of your cells copies it with near-perfect accuracy.

Every single cell in your body has about two meters of DNA packed inside its nucleus. And every time that cell divides, it has to copy all two meters of it — and it does that with about one error per a billion bases. How? That is the question for today. The answer is a molecular assembly line of about seven proteins working in a precise order.

Three things to lock in. First — the structure of DNA itself: double helix, antiparallel strands, base pairing. Second — replication: semiconservative, leading versus lagging strand, and the lineup of enzymes that do the work. Third — proofreading and repair, plus telomeres and why they matter for aging and cancer.

Nineteen fifty-three. Watson and Crick publish the double helix model — but they did it using Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction data, which she took without their knowledge. The structure is two strands twisted around each other. The backbone is alternating sugar and phosphate — deoxyribose plus phosphate. The bases stick inward and pair up across the middle. And the strands are antiparallel — one runs five-prime to three-prime, the other runs three-prime to five-prime. Major and minor grooves on the outside let proteins read the sequence without unwinding.

Chargaff noticed something weird before anyone knew the structure — in any DNA sample, the percentage of A always equals the percentage of T, and the percentage of G equals the percentage of C. That was a huge clue. The reason: A pairs with T using two hydrogen bonds, and G pairs with C using three hydrogen bonds. That extra bond means G-C rich regions are harder to pull apart — which matters when an enzyme tries to unwind the helix.

How does DNA copy itself? Three guesses were possible — conservative, semiconservative, or dispersive. In nineteen fifty-eight, Meselson and Stahl settled it. They grew bacteria in heavy nitrogen, switched to light nitrogen, and tracked DNA density after each round of replication. The result matched only one model — semiconservative. Each new double helix has one original strand and one brand-new strand. The old strand serves as the template for the new one.

The first four enzymes set up the fork. Helicase unwinds the double helix, breaking the hydrogen bonds between bases. Single-strand binding proteins, or SSBPs, coat the separated strands so they don't snap back together. Topoisomerase works ahead of the fork — it cuts and rejoins the DNA to relieve the supercoiling that builds up from unwinding. Then primase lays down a short RNA primer, because DNA polymerase cannot start from scratch — it needs an existing three-prime OH to build onto.

Now the main player. DNA polymerase III adds nucleotides only in the five-prime to three-prime direction. Always. This creates an asymmetry. On the leading strand, polymerase moves toward the fork and synthesizes one continuous piece. On the lagging strand, the template runs the wrong way, so polymerase has to work backward — in short pieces called Okazaki fragments — each one needing its own RNA primer. Both strands are still built five-prime to three-prime — that part never changes.

After Pol three lays down the new DNA, two cleanup enzymes finish the job. DNA polymerase one removes the RNA primers and replaces them with DNA — same five-prime to three-prime direction. But that leaves tiny nicks between the new fragments. DNA ligase seals those nicks by forming the final phosphodiester bond, joining everything into one continuous strand.

Try this one. Given the template strand three-prime — T A C G G T — five-prime, write the new strand that DNA polymerase builds. And which direction did polymerase build it? Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Answer. Pair each base — A with T, G with C. T pairs with A, A pairs with T, C with G, G with C, G with C, T with A. The new strand is five-prime — A T G C C A — three-prime. And polymerase built it five-prime to three-prime, reading the template in the three-prime to five-prime direction. That five-prime to three-prime synthesis rule is the source of every quirky thing about replication — including the lagging strand and Okazaki fragments.

How does the cell get to one error per a billion bases? Three layers. First — DNA polymerase has built-in three-prime to five-prime exonuclease activity. As it builds, it can back up and remove a mismatched base it just put down. Second — mismatch repair proteins patrol behind and fix errors that slipped past proofreading. Third — nucleotide excision repair handles bulky damage, especially the thymine dimers caused by UV light. People with xeroderma pigmentosum have a broken excision repair pathway, which is why they get skin cancer from even small amounts of sunlight.

Now an awkward problem. Linear chromosome ends — telomeres — have a repeating sequence, in humans it is T-T-A-G-G-G repeated thousands of times. Every time the lagging strand is replicated, the very end can't be fully copied — there is no room for a final primer. So chromosomes shorten a little with every division. This is the end replication problem. Telomerase, an enzyme with its own RNA template, can extend telomeres back out — but in your body, only germ cells and stem cells make active telomerase. Most adult cells don't, which is part of why we age. And cancer cells almost always reactivate telomerase — that is what makes them effectively immortal.

Common traps on this topic. Trap one — students think the lagging strand is built three-prime to five-prime. It is not. Both strands are built five-prime to three-prime — the lagging one just does it in short backward chunks. Trap two — DNA polymerase cannot start on its own. It needs a primer. Always primase first, polymerase second. Trap three — semiconservative is not the same as conservative or dispersive. Semiconservative means one old strand plus one new strand per daughter helix. Get those three traps right and you have most of the points on this topic.

Recap. DNA is a double helix, antiparallel strands, A pairs with T using two bonds, G pairs with C using three bonds. Replication is semiconservative — Meselson and Stahl proved it. The enzyme order matters: helicase, SSBPs, topoisomerase, primase, then DNA polymerase three building five-prime to three-prime — continuous on the leading strand, Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand. Polymerase one swaps RNA primer for DNA. Ligase seals the nicks. Proofreading plus mismatch repair plus nucleotide excision repair get error rates down to one in a billion. Telomeres shorten with age; telomerase rebuilds them and is reactivated in cancer.

Your path forward. Read OpenStax Biology, Chapter Fourteen, for the deeper version of replication. Drill the Khan Academy AP Biology Unit Six problem sets — questions about the leading versus lagging strand and the enzyme order come up almost every year. Do the assignment in your dashboard. Book an advisor check-in if the directionality of polymerase still feels confusing. Next time — Module Eight, Transcription and Translation. We go from DNA to RNA to protein.
